title: Advanced microfluidic tool for rapid buffer exchange and in situ imaging of phase separation in immobilized giant unilamellar vesicles
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"pdbjy","text":"Semi-permeable Giant Unilamellar Vesicles (GUVs) are powerful tools for studying macromolecular assembly, notably liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS). Here, we present a microfluidic protocol for GUVs production, immobilization and rapid buffer exchange. It enables real-time imaging of phase separation within isolated GUVs with various microscopy technics (optical, confocal and UV-autofluorescence). The principle of this device is based on the production of water-in-oil-in-water double emulsion droplets and their turning into GUVs by elimination of their oil phase. The immobilization of GUVs is provided by physical and chemical traps, using the strong biotin-streptavidin affinity. These vesicles become semi-permeable to small molecules (\u003c 2000 g/mol) viathe α-hemolysin pore protein. Within minutes, the internal GUV phase can be changed using different external buffers. This provides a way to induce the assembly of macromolecules and monitor overtime the dynamic of phase separation. In particular, we show, on a specific example, the occurrence of LLPS through both nucleation and growth and spinodal decomposition. The protocol can be divided into two distinct phases: (1) the chip fabrication spans approximately two days but yields approximately 10 chips, and (2) the GUV production, immobilization, and buffer exchange, can be completed in just half an hour. This microfluidic tool represents a significant advance by offering a faster and more controlled buffer exchange as compared to alternative methods. It opens new routes to investigate the dynamics of macromolecular assembly, particularly in the context of LLPS.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":763,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tzjrb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"dtjjw","text":"Use of GUVs to probe macromolecule assembly.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":44}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"utegu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hxyto","text":"Giant Unilamellar Vesicles (GUVs) are valuable tools for investigating dynamic macromolecular assembly, such as liquid-liquid phase separation, under varying physicochemical conditions. Unlike conventional liposome production methods, which often yield low encapsulation rates and polydisperse vesicles, droplet-based microfluidics has greatly improved GUVs production (Chariot, Sanchez, Roux, \u0026 Teixeira, 2014; Deshpande, Caspi, Meijering, \u0026 Dekker, 2016; Petit, Polenz, Baret, Herminghaus, \u0026 Bäumchen, 2016; Shum, Lee, Yoon, Kodger, \u0026 Weitz, 2008; Teh, Khnouf, Fan, \u0026 Lee, 2011; Walde, Cosentino, Engel, \u0026 Stano, 2010). The process involves creating water-in-oil-in-water (w/o/w) double emulsions through fluid shearing and subsequently removing the lipid-containing oil phase from the droplets through budding-off, evaporation, or solubilization in the external phase. This results in GUVs formation, with the potential to incorporate nano-pores into their lipid bilayers, enabling semi-permeability. For example, the use of α-hemolysin (α-HL) allows the exchange of small molecules (\u003c 2 kDa) across the membrane bilayer of GUVs (Bayley, 1997; Song et al., 1996). Such vesicles serve as cell-like microreactors facilitating the study of subcellular dynamics and offering promising applications in drug delivery (Deshpande et al., 2016; Göpfrich et al., 2019; Last, Deshpande, \u0026 Dekker, 2020; Noireaux \u0026 Libchaber, 2004a; Robinson, Kuhn, Eyer, \u0026 Dittrich, 2013; Song et al., 1996; Tsuji et al., 2013). However, the main limitations of GUVs produced using previous microfluidic approaches are the slow buffer exchange rate (Deshpande et al., 2016; Noireaux \u0026 Libchaber, 2004b; Robinson et al., 2013) and the lack of control of the such exchange. This limitation hindered their suitability for probing the dynamics of macromolecules assembly in response to changing physicochemical conditions.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2h4vu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a75ru","text":"Development of the protocol","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":27}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3xcko","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pub8s","text":"To address these challenges, we developed a new microfluidic tool. This tool not only produces quickly monodisperse GUVs with high encapsulation yield but also immobilizes GUVs. It facilitates rapid buffer exchange and enables in situ imaging. In particular, we took inspiration from previous work that spatially confine droplets through physical traps within microfluidic chips (Huebner et al., 2009; Nguyen, Marquis, Anton, \u0026 Marze, 2019; Nilsson, Evander, Hammarström, \u0026 Laurell, 2009; Robinson et al., 2013; Shi, Qin, Ye, \u0026 Lin, 2008; Tan \u0026 Takeuchi, 2007; Xu, Sarder, Li, \u0026 Nehorai, 2013). This provides an efficient way to replace the external phase of GUVs after production. The proof of concept of this microfluidic approach was published in a first publication (Cochereau et al., 2020). Our latest developments include the adaptation of the microfluidic chip for compatibility with confocal microscopy and UV-synchrotron microscopy. This implies immobilising the GUVs for proper imaging based on chemical bonding of the GUVs to the PDMS traps using with biotinylated-BSA and streptavidin/biotinylated-lipids (Eyer et al., 2014; Kuhn et al., 2012; Münter, Andresen, \u0026 Larsen, 2019) and adapting the chip to glass/quartz coverslip. This improved chip has enabled us to study the phase separation kinetics of wheat gliadins in a recent paper (ref future).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":227,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"micsp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4tcru","text":"Applications of the method","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":26}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xvbvu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"afub1","text":"This chip has been designed to study kinetics of macromolecule assembly at a scale larger than 1 µm while offering precise control over physicochemical conditions. The formulation of GUVs can be easily adapted to user needs. For example, the lipid composition can be modulated to study, for example, the interplay between LLPS and membrane interactions (Last et al., 2020). Additionally, adjustments in pore size and shape, such as using origami pores (Cochereau et al., 2023; Fragasso et al., 2021; Groeer, Garni, Samanta, \u0026 Walther, 2022; Krishnan et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2023) can be implemented to further customize the microenvironment for specific experiments.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hbaog","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kkyyx","text":"Beyond its application in the context of macromolecule assembly and LLPS, semi-permeable GUVs can serve as continuous reactors for chemical/biochemical reactions that requires confined environement, like protein synthesis (Noireaux \u0026 Libchaber, 2004). The specific advance of the chip is the possibility to module, overtime, the feeding content.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"esjuw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ggnon","text":"Principle of the method","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":23}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bi6ux","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qygou","text":"The principle of this device is represented in Figure 1. The first part of the chip allows the fast production (\u003c 1s) of a water/oil/water (w/o/w) emulsion in a flow focusing area, by injection of the three following phases: (1) an outer aqueous phase (OF), a middle fluid phase (MF) composed of phospholipids (2) and (3) an inner aqueous fluid phase (IF) (Figure 1a). In the second part of the chip, physical traps are used to isolate the w/o/w emulsions droplets and to remove their oil phase in a few minutes (~20 min) by the action of a high OF flow (Figure 1b). Lipids interfaces assembly turns the w/o/w emulsions droplets into GUVs. By injecting a solution of α-HL, GUVs then become semi-permeable in less than 5 minutes (Figure 1c). As presented in the associated published work (Cochereau et al., 2020), this tool provides an efficient way to study solvent-triggered macromolecular assembly within isolated GUVs.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2vwol","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"lvzeg","text":"\u003ch1\u003eOverview of the method\u003c/h1\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gvjxo","text":"\u003ch2\u003eMicrofluidic chip design and fabrication\u003c/h2\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hnlbw","text":"The developed microfluidic device design, represented in Figure 2, was inspired by recent works (Nguyen et al., 2019; Petit et al., 2016) and is composed of 3 inlets, 2 outlets, a double flow focusing geometry and a connected trapping chamber. A representation with detailed dimensions of the microfluidic chip is available in Figure S1.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e6rbi","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"k4tzx","text":"  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gpf5u","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d9ulq","text":"According to the method of (Nguyen et al., 2019) with slight modifications, we used a soft lithography (Xia \u0026 Whitesides, 1998) technic to produce it as represented in Figure 3. Briefly, this technic consists in the fabrication of a mold master of the device by spin coating, UV exposure and development of a photoresist resin (e.g. SU-8). This mold master is then used to fabricate the microfluidic chip by PDMS molding and bonding. This technic of chips fabrications allows a pattern resolution of micrometers range (Kim et al., 2008). In comparison with other technics such as glass etching or 3D printing, it is relatively cheap, easy to perform and flexible (Niculescu, Chircov, Bîrcă, \u0026 Grumezescu, 2021).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"0denw","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"euxk6","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xextd","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"glgrt","text":"\u003ch2\u003eMicrofluidic chip surface treatments\u003c/h2\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"uafdx","text":"Due to the PDMS hydrophobicity (Mata, Fleischman, \u0026 Roy, 2005), the wettability of the PDMS chips' channels by water is not optimal. Consequently, we carried out a partial surface treatment of channels as represented in Figure 4a. Chips are made partially hydrophilic with this treatment. This provides to form w/o/w emulsions. We used a technic based on the grafting of poly(acrylate) on the PDMS surface by a photoradical reaction using benzophenone and acrylic acid (Schneider, Willaime, Tran, Rezgui, \u0026 Tabeling, 2010). As described in Figure 4b, after the surface treatment, the PDMS surface is covered by hydrophilic molecules. This technic is similar to other technics of hydrophilic surface treatment that use for example poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) to coat PDMS channels surfaces (Trantidou, Elani, Parsons, \u0026 Ces, 2017). As described in the literature (Deshpande \u0026 Dekker, 2018), this type of channels treatment is a key step in the production of microfluidics devices which allows production of w/o/w droplets.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7uogp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"idnia","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8b7pv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"iuvie","text":"The last step in the production of our microfluidic device consists in chemically binding the phospholipids at the surface of the trapping chamber channels. Despite the presence of traps, liposomes are mobile \"on-site\" due to the OF flow. This surface treatment was performed to enhance efficiency of the trapping of GUVs. It is based on the strong affinity of biotin with streptavidin (Kd ~ 10−14 M) (Green, 1975). It consists in depositing biotined bovine serum albumin (BSA) and streptavidin onto channels and to use biotined lipids in the composition of GUVs, as described in Figure 5. The protocol used for this treatment was inspired by literature and adapted to our device (Eyer et al., 2014; Kuhn et al., 2012; Münter et al., 2019).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":394,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":388,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"v92m6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"u49qc","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"yksm3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"q5xip","text":"\u003ch2\u003eMicrofluidic chip optimizations\u003c/h2\u003e","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"kwbdl","text":"Finally, we have adapted this device for confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and for UV-autofluorescence microscopy. The bottom of the classical PDMS/PDMS chips generally presents a minimum thickness comprised between 1 and 5 mm, as represented in Figure 6a. Some microscopic techniques require thinner supports to achieve, for example, z-sections observations of a few microns. It is the case of the CLSM and of the UV-autofluorescence microscopy. Glass and quartz microscope coverslips (thickness ~0.1 mm) were used to respectively proceed at an optimization of the chips for CLSM and UV-autofluorescence technics experiments. The replacement of the bottom of the chips by these materials, as represented in Figure 6b-c, provides deeper visualization into the chips. Quartz coverslip was chosen for the circuits used in UV-autofluorescence because they present higher transparency to radiation in the UV-wavelength range. Due to their fragility, quartz coverslips present small dimensions (Ø = 25 mm). The dimension of the circuit for UV-autofluorescence thus has to be reduced by a factor of ~5. This is the reason why this device has been divided into two distinct and connected microfluidic chips. The first one entirely in PDMS is presented in Figures 2 and 6a; it allows the production of double emulsions. The second one, in PDMS and quartz, allows the trapping and the analysis of droplets. The latter is prepared with an adapted protocol that also allows the fabrication of microfluidics devices in PDMS and glass (presented in figure 6b) adapted for CLSM experiments.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"aj0wa","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7u48c","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: To prepare the benzophenone solution used for hydrophilic surface treatment of the microfluidic chip, disperse benzophenone at 10 %wt in acetone. A gentle agitation for ten seconds is enough to obtain its complete solubilization. Due to its toxicity, proceed for the weighing and the solubilization under a fume hood and wear appropriate protective equipment. This solution has to be prepared before each surface treatment experiment. As acetone is highly volatile solvent, the transfer of this solution to the syringe used for its injection in the treated microfluidic chips must be realized as soon as the benzophenone is solubilized. 5 mL of the solution is enough to proceed to the treatment of about ten microfluidics chips.

The acrylic acid solution used for hydrophilic surface treatment of the microfluidic device is prepared by dilution of acrylic acid at 20 %wt in Milli-Q ultra-pure water. Acrylic acid is highly corrosive and flammable. Handle it under a fume hood and wear appropriate protective equipment. 5 mL is enough to proceed to the treatment of about ten microfluidics chips.

A mixture of BSA and biotinylated BSA is used to cover microfluidics chips of chemical anchor points for GUVs immobilization. To obtain it, solubilize by magnetic stirring for 1h, BSA and biotinylated BSA powders at respectively 90 %mol and 10 %mol in a 10 mM HEPES aqueous buffer, containing 95 mM of NaCl. Less than 500 µL of this solution is needed to proceed to the entire treatment of about ten microfluidic chips. This solution is stored at 4 °C for ~ 10 days.

The streptavidin solution used for chemical immobilization of GUVs is prepared by solubilization of streptavidin powder at 25 µg/mL in a 10 mM HEPES aqueous buffer, containing 95 mM of NaCl. A magnetic stirring of 1h is sufficient to perform this solubilization. Less than 500 µL of this solution is needed to proceed to the entire treatment of about ten microfluidic chips. As recommended by the supplier, this solution is aliquoted and stored at -20 °C for several months or at 4°C for several weeks.

The inner fluid phase consists of Milli-Q ultra-purified water. IF have to be filtered with a 0.2 µm filter before injection to avoid dusts and bubbles, which can clog channels and led to changes in pressure and flow directions. Fluorescent probes such as calcein can be dispersed in IF for fluorescence microscopy visualizations. To do so, dispense the appropriate amount of calcein into water, so that the final concentration is 10 µM. To perform an experiment of GUVs formation with the microfluidic device, less than 200 µl of solution is needed. To avoid bacterial growth, it is recommended to store IF at 4°C.

The middle fluid phase is composed of DOPC, DOPG, DSPE-PEG-biotin lipids, dispersed in oleic acid with the respectively molar ratio 98.9:1:0.1 and a total lipid concentration of 6.5 mM. The following stock solutions are prepared by solubilization of lipids in oleic acid: - Dissolve DOPC (MW = 786.113 g/mol) to 10 mM. - Dissolve DOPG (MW = 797.026 g/mol) to 1 mM. - Dissolve DSPE-PEG-Biotin (MW = 3016.781 g/mol) to 0.1 mM. Note: Use an ultrasonic bath at the maximum amplitude for 2 h at 30 °C to completely dissolve lipids within oleic acid. Nile red (10 ppm) hydrophobic fluorescent probe can be added to the lipid mixture for fluorescence microscopy experiments to visualize the MF. In this case, the mixture is sonicated for 2 h at 30 °C in the bath sonicator after the probe addition. These lipid stock solutions can be stored at -20°C for months. Then, mix the stock solutions to a molar ratio DOPC:DOPG:DSPE-PEG-biotin of 98.9:1:0.1. This final lipid dispersion can be used as MF for GUVs preparation, after sonication at 30°C for 2h. It can be stored and used to produce stable emulsion droplets, for several weeks at room temperature.

The outer fluid phase consists of a mixture of 10 %wt ethanol and 20 %wt glycerol. Glycerol is used to increase the viscosity of the OF, which increases the w/o/w emulsion droplets formation at the second flow focusing junction. Ethanol is used to solubilize the oleic acid of the double emulsions droplets that enhances the GUVs formation in the trapping chamber. To prepare it, mix ethanol and glycerol in the suitable proportions and proceed to an overnight gentle agitation of the mixture. The resulting solution can be used to produce stable emulsion droplets for one month by storing it at 4 °C. Other chemicals components can be added in the OF phase. For example, amphiphilic copolymers, such as Pluronics, can be used to enhance droplets stability (Chariot et al., 2014; Deng, Yelleswarapu, & Huck, 2016; Deshpande et al., 2016; Teh et al., 2011). Buffer molecules can also be added to this phase to control the vesicles pH.

To prepare the α-hemolysin solution, used for pore formation within lipids bilayers, disperse it at 2 mM in ultrapure water, or in its desired aqueous buffer of use, by magnetic stirring for 1 h. The resulting solution has to be filtered with a 0.2 µm filter to remove α-HL aggregates before its injection in a microfluidics device. This solution is aliquoted and stored at -20 °C in dark conditions for several months. Each aliquot can be used in a day after defrosting to avoid α-HL aggregation or degradation.

The setup for GUVs production, immobilization, and microscopic analysis is outlined in Figure 7. Briefly, the microfluidic chip, presented in Figure 2, is placed on a sample holder of an inverted microscope, connected to fluorescent and light sources and to a camera linked to a computer. GUVs are produced in this device by injections of IF, MF and OF phases contained in syringes placed on three different syringe pumps. These syringes are connected to the inputs of the microfluidic device using Tygon tubing, luer stubs, and stainless steel couplers. Microscopic images of droplets and channels are recorded by the use of an image acquisition software. Note: An inverted microscope is more suitable than a straight microscope since it provides large free space on the top of the chips. This is suitable for the tubing inputs of the microfluidic device. CAUTION! For better reproducibility in the droplets production, it is recommended to keep fixed the position of the syringes, the length of the tubing, and the position of the microscope from one experiment to another.

For a sake of clarity, we first present the procedure concerning the production and use of "classical" PDMS/PDMS chips. The optimization of this procedure for CLSM and UV-autofluorescence microscopy (PDMS/glass, PDMS/quartz chips) is presented at the end of this part in IV.7.

Use a Computer-Aided Design (CAD) drawing software such as Adobe Illustrator to design the patterns of the microfluidic device. Note: The dimensions of the microfluidic circuit drawing are detailed in the supplementary information part in Figure S1.

Save the drawing in negative format as vector graphic (e.g. .ai file extension). The channels drew in black on white have to appear in white on black. ▲ CRITICAL STEP A vector graphic format of the drawing is essential to obtain a high-resolution mask in the next steps.

Order this drawing to a printing service to obtain an opaque black mask on which the channels appear transparent. ▲ CRITICAL STEP Several masks from different printing services were tested in this study. It was observed that a minimum printing resolution of 3000 d.p.i. is necessary for the fabrication of the small patterns of the microfluidic chip. Furthermore, the mask must be completely opaque at its black areas; otherwise, unwanted partial crosslinking of the SU-8 resin could take place during the UV-insolation. The opacity of the mask can be measured with a reflectometer. We used a mask printed at 4000 d.p.i. by the company Micro Lithography Services (U.K.). This mask presents a good resolution and a high level of opacity of the black areas.

▲ CRITICAL STEP The presence of dust in the working environment in steps 4-25 seriously affects the quality of the obtained microfluidic chips. If possible, carry out these steps in a clean room (ISO 5-8). Otherwise, perform these experiments after removing dusts from surfaces and utensils used by passing a pressurized airflow and by working under a laminar flow hood.

Clean a 100 mm diameter silicon wafer by the use of a Kimtech wipe soaked in isopropanol and of a stream of pressurized air. This is done to eliminate the major part of the impurities that may be present on the surface.

Protect the inside of the spin coater against resin splashes with a protection liner. Then, center the wafer into the spin coater, activate the vacuum to fix it and proceed to the spin coating of approximatively 10 g of SU-8 2100 resin, at 2000 rpm for 35s with a ramp of 500 rpm.s -1 . This leads to a resin thickness of ~ 140 µm as determined by profilometry experiments (data not shown). Until development of the master (step 10), the wafer with the resin must be protected as much as possible from any light source. Note: Different resin thicknesses can be obtained by changing the spin coating duration. The longer the spin coating, the thinner the resin. Another more or less viscous resin can also be chosen to obtain a different thickness. For the same spin coating conditions (duration, speed), the more viscous the resin, the thicker it is. ▲ CRITICAL STEP A minimum resin thickness of 100 µm is needed to obtain well-defined traps geometries on the master in the later steps (resin development). ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Immediately, proceed to the prebaking of the spin-coated wafer by placing it on a 65°C ceramic hotplate for 5 min. Then, place it on a 95°C ceramic hot plate for 20 min.
Cool down the spin-coated wafer to room temperature and place it on the wafer support of a photolithography UV insolator (e.g. UV KUB 2).

Place the mask obtained in step 3 in contact with the top of the spin-coated wafer and UV expose the resin through the mask at a power per surface of 35mW/cm² and a wavelength of 365 nm for 15 s. ▲ CRITICAL STEP The insolation duration and the position of the mask are critical for the resolution of the pattern. Optimal conditions were observed for exposition duration below 20 s and hard contact conditions (masking distance = 0 um). Longer exposition duration induces resin crosslink in unexposed areas. ? TROUBLESHOOTING

Immediately, proceed to the postbaking of the insolated spin-coated wafer by placing it on a 65°C ceramic hotplate for 5min. Then, place it on a 95°C ceramic hot plate for 10 min. Note: The reticulated resin representing the design must be visible in transparency. ? TROUBLESHOOTING

Cool down the spin-coated wafer to room temperature and place it in a bath of SU-8 developer (~ 200 mL) under manual gentle agitation for ~ 35-45 min. Renew the bath after 15 min of development. Wash the well-developed wafer with isopropanol and pass a pressurized air stream to dry it and to obtain the desired master. CAUTION ! SU-8 Developer is highly flammable, volatile and toxic. Manipulate it under a fume hood and wear appropriate protective equipments. ▲ CRITICAL STEP The increase in development duration induces better solubilization of the non-photocrosslinked resin. However, for durations > 45min, the crosslinked resin may crack. It is necessary to check the development of the resin when designing very small patterns, by successive observations with a stereoscopic microscope. ? TROUBLESHOOTING ☐ PAUSE POINT The resulting master can be stored indefinitely at room temperature in a plastic Petri dish. Dust must be removed with a stream of pressurized air before the following steps.

Place the master obtained in step 10 on a piece of aluminium foil in a plastic Petri dish. Arrange the foil to create borders around the master; this will be used to contain the poured PDMS in the next steps.

Prepare two PDMS/crosslinker mixtures in disposable beakers, a first 30 g batch with 10 % wt of crosslinker and another of 20 g with 5 % wt of crosslinker. Each batch is vigorously mixed with a rigid utensil such as a pipette transfer to obtain a homogenous mixture. The resulting mixture is turbid due to the presence of incorporated air bubbles which will be removed under vacuum in step 14. CAUTION ! A surface covered with uncrosslinked PDMS is very difficult to clean. It is therefore recommended to protect the surfaces and to wear appropriate protective equipments. ▲ CRITICAL STEP It is recommended to vigorously mix this mixture for at least 2-3 minutes. The insufficient mixing gives inhomogeneous crosslinked PDMS after the subsequent heating step. When insufficient crosslinked, PDMS can clog the channels during the assembly of the chip pieces. Contrarily, if it is excessively crosslinked, it cannot be stuck to the PDMS bottom (step 20) leading to leaks problems. The resolution of the pattern was found to be sensitive to PDMS storage conditions. We recommend to aliquot it in a few hundred grams. Store it at 4°C and use each aliquot rapidly after opening (~ 1-4 weeks). ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Pass a pressurized airflow on the surface of the previously prepared master to eliminate dusts.

Under a laminar flow hood, pour the 10 %wt PDMS mixture into the master and the 5 %wt PDMS mixture into a Petri dish before placing them in a vacuum desiccator for 2 h, at pressure around 30 mBar, until the bubbles disappear completely. ▲ CRITICAL STEP The presence of bubbles on the surface of the PDMS at the end of this step must be avoided. It leads to defects in the finals microfluidics devices, which can generate visual distortions of the patterns during microscopic observations. ? TROUBLESHOOTING

Heat the master and the Petri dish, which contains PDMS in a 70°C oven for 20 min. Then let them cool to room temperature for 5 min under a laminar flow hood. ▲ CRITICAL STEP The resulting PDMS does not have to be completely crosslinked. You must be able to bend it on itself without breaking or deforming it. As mentioned in the critical step of the point 12, the optimal crosslinking of PDMS is essential to obtain a usable microfluidic device. ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Under a laminar flow hood, cut with a scalpel and gently peel the PDMS slab from the master. ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Using tape, remove the small residual pieces of PDMS that remained stuck to the cut PDMS slab.

Using a biopsy puncher (ID = 0.50 mm), create holes in the inlets and outlets patterns of the PDMS slab placed flatly on a cutting mat. ▲ CRITICAL STEP Punching must be done vertically in one go and the punched part must absolutely be completely removed from the PDMS slab. A non-vertical punching of the inlets and outlets can lead to leaks during the use of the microfluidic chips. In addition, the presence of residual PDMS pieces can block the channels of the chips. ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Using tape, remove the small residual pieces of PDMS that remained stuck after punching.

Put the PDMS slab in contact with the crosslinked PDMS that remained in the Petri dish and place them in a 70°C oven for 1 hour. ▲ CRITICAL STEP Position the PDMS slab carefully on the other PDMS piece from one edge to another. Note: The patterns must be visible highlighted in backlight. ? TROUBLESHOOTING
Let cool the resulting two-part PDMS microfluidic chip to room temperature for 5 min, under a laminar flow hood.

Insert stainless steel plugs (ID = 0.5 mm) in the punched inlets and outlets. ▲ CRITICAL STEP For the same reason of the critical step of the point 18, the insertion of the plugs must be done vertically in one go. ? TROUBLESHOOTING

In this work, we present a protocol for the fabrication and utilization of PDMS microfluidic device tailored for the production of immobilized Giant Unilamellar Vesicles (GUVs) with rapid buffer exchange capabilities and in situ imaging using various microscopy techniques. During the microlithography and PDMS molding steps, a crucial aspect of this protocol is the precise definition of the circuit pattern. The success of these steps relies on the quality of the mask, the crosslinking and development of the SU-8 resin, and the crosslinking and assembly of PDMS components. Additionally, surface treatments play a crucial role in rendering the chip partially hydrophilic and chemically immobilizing GUVs. The effectiveness of these treatments is linked to the meticulous preparation of treatment solutions and their precise flow within the microfluidic chips. Finally, the duration, sequence, and flow rates of the injected fluid phases within the chip are key parameters to master the execution of this protocol. The resulting microfluidic tool is expected to produces size-controlled w/o/w double emulsion droplets, as shown in Figure 8a. These double-emulsion droplets are efficiently immobilized and isolated within a chamber equipped with physical traps (Figure 8b). Remarkably, our methodology facilitates the transformation of these immobilized double-emulsion droplets into GUVs by removing oil in less than 20 minutes (Figure 8b-d), a significant improvement compared to the more than 10 hours typically required for bulk oil solubilization (Petit et al., 2016; Teh et al., 2011). Notably, in our method, oil removal does not only rely on solubilization but is also attributed to detachment facilitated by the high shear forces generated by the middle fluid (MF) phase acting on the outer fluid (OF) phase. The method is, however, longer than the octanol-assisted one (Deshpande & Dekker, 2018). The trapping chamber is then used to turn GUVs into semi-permeable GUVs by flowing in a pore-forming protein, like α-hemolysin. The permeability of the GUVs is illustrated by calcein leakage in Figure 8e. These exchanges provide an efficient way to tune the physicochemical conditions within GUVs. As presented in our published work (Cochereau et al., 2020), this tool can be used to modulate the GUVs internal pH. It takes less than one minute to go from 4.0 to a value of 7.0. In literature, it is reported that this type of molecules exchanging is generally performed in 5 to 120 min (Deshpande et al., 2016; Robinson et al., 2013; Teh et al., 2011). The microfluidic chip described here therefore provides faster buffer exchanges probably due to the continuous renewing of the outer phase in the trapping chamber. Also, the trapping chamber is adapted to study the reversibility of phenomena which is a crucial thermodynamic information. We have also presented a detailed optimized protocol for the production of similar PDMS/glass and PDMS/quartz microfluidics chips. The principal key step of this optimized protocol consists of bonding the PDMS onto a glass or quartz coverslip. To do so, oxygen plasma must be performed in good conditions (pressure, duration of plasma treatment, heating …). Replacing the bottom of the classical chip with a glass coverslip allows z-sections fluorescent observations in CLSM. As shown in Figure 9a, this provide 3D images reconstruction of GUVs. Replacing it with quartz coverslip enable using UV-radiations for autofluorescence microscopy imaging of encapsulated molecules, as presented in Figure 9b. Beyond the case study of GUVs, it was successfully used to probe interaction between proteins and cellulose nanocrystals using UV-autofluorescence microscopy (Voisin et al., 2023) and to follow lipid droplet digestions using light microscopy and CLSM (Marze, Algaba, & Marquis, 2014; Nguyen et al., 2019).
